parents.father_research_notes: William Shedden — loyalist merchant, New York; illegitimate father

notable.notable_blurb: Litchfield Female Academy student and music teacher

narrative_blocks.header: Illegitimate daughter of a loyalist merchant
narrative_blocks.detail: William Shedden fled to England after the Revolution; his two illegitimate daughters — Annabella and Jane, born of different mothers — were raised in the new republic he had opposed. Annabella's path from loyalist's daughter to teacher at one of early America's most celebrated female academies is a quiet study in reinvention. The Litchfield Female Academy, run by Sarah Pierce, educated hundreds of women from prominent families across the early republic. Annabella's time there as both student and instructor placed her at the center of that world. (Research notes; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: Annabella was the illegitimate daughter of William Shedden, a prominent pre-Revolutionary loyalist merchant in New York. She attended the Bethlehem Female Seminary in 1798 and the Litchfield Female Academy around 1803–04, then returned to teach music there from 1805 to 1808.

narrative_blocks.header: Married the founder of Litchfield Law School's only
narrative_blocks.detail: The son, Tapping Burr Reeve (HD1912), was named for his paternal grandfather — Judge Tapping Reeve, the man who had opposed his parents' marriage. He died at 20 in Litchfield in 1829, the last of the Reeve line. Annabella later married David Judson Burr Sr. (X01882) in Litchfield in 1812 and moved to Richmond, Virginia, where she died in 1849. Her children from the second marriage were not Hooker descendants. (Research notes; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: In November 1808 Annabella married Aaron Burr Reeve, only son of Judge Tapping Reeve — founder of the Litchfield Law School and uncle of Aaron Burr. The Reeve family initially opposed the match because of Annabella's illegitimate birth. Aaron died nine months later, leaving Annabella a widow with an infant son.
